fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that document database client adapter we've been talking about. Basically devs on our side keep complaining that they're hand-rolling all these weird nested path strings and type envelopes every time they touch the database layer, and it's causing a ton of subtle bugs in production — wrong paths, broken type conversions, that kind of thing. We want a clean library that handles all of this for them.

The library needs to cover the usual stuff: building and parsing those long resource name strings (you know, the wire prefix format we use), handling nested field paths safely (including the escaping logic — refer to how we handled special characters in the query builder from the last sprint), converting between our internal value types and what the wire actually expects, reading document snapshots without accidentally mutating them, navigating between collections and documents, putting together structured queries with filters/ordering/limits/cursors, and batching up write operations like creates, sets, updates, deletes, and transforms.

The whole thing should be driven by a JSON command interface so we can black-box test it easily. Output needs to be pretty-printed JSON. Error cases should be handled gracefully, not just crash. Multi-file structure please, this is getting complex enough that a single god-file would be a nightmare to maintain. Python is fine.

trigger_keywords: resource path format
what does the wire prefix look like
how should absolute document names be structured
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Absolute document paths must use the format `projects/<project>/databases/<database>/documents/<relative_path>`. Database names use `projects/<project>/databases/<database>`. The `append_to_database` operation takes an existing absolute database name and appends `/documents/<relative_path>` to it. Relative path extraction strips the `projects/<project>/databases/<database>/documents/` prefix.

trigger_keywords: field path escaping rules
when do segments need backtick quoting
which characters require escaping in wire masks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Field path segments must be backtick-escaped in wire mask strings only when they contain characters outside `[a-zA-Z0-9_]` or when they start with a digit. Simple alphanumeric segments are written as-is with dot separators. The `segments_to_path` operation returns both `segments` and `path` as arrays of the segment strings (not a dotted string). The `string_to_segments` operation returns the original dotted string under `path` and the split list under `segments`.

trigger_keywords: how are wire value types decoded
what does a document reference decode to
how should referenceValue be represented in the output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A `referenceValue` wire field decodes to an object with keys `type` (always `"document_reference"`), `name` (the full absolute path), `path` (the relative path after the documents prefix), `id` (the last segment of the relative path), and `parent` (the absolute path of the parent collection, i.e., everything up to but not including the last segment). Timestamps decode to `{"kind": "timestamp", "seconds": <int>, "nanos": <int>}`. GeoPoints decode to `{"type": "geo_point", "latitude": <float>, "longitude": <float>}`. Bytes decode to `{"type": "bytes", "value": <base64string>}`.

trigger_keywords: snapshot identity fields
what fields does the identity operation return
how do I get the document id and path from a snapshot
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The snapshot `identity` operation returns exactly four fields: `name` (the full absolute document name), `path` (the relative path below the documents root), `id` (the last segment of the relative path), and `exists` (boolean). The `timestamps` operation returns `readTime`, `updateTime`, and `createTime` each as timestamp descriptor objects with `kind`, `seconds`, and `nanos`. The `fields` operation returns the document's field data nested under a `fields` key.

trigger_keywords: collection vs document reference identity
what is the difference between collection_identity and document_identity
does a collection reference have a parent field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A `collection_identity` result contains `type` (`"collection_reference"`), `name`, `path`, and `id` — it does NOT include a `parent` field. A `document_identity` result contains `type` (`"document_reference"`), `name`, `path`, `id`, AND `parent` (the absolute path of the containing collection, i.e., all segments except the last).

trigger_keywords: how do multiple select steps get merged
query select accumulation behavior
do multiple where clauses combine into one structured query
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Multiple `select` steps within a single query command are accumulated: all field paths from all select steps are merged into a single `select.fields` list in the final `structuredQuery`, in the order they were added. Multiple `where` steps each add an independent filter to a composite `ANDFILTER` in `structuredQuery.where.compositeFilter.filters`. The operator `=` maps to `EQUAL` in the wire format. The output always includes `calls` (the list of observed database requests), `rows` (decoded snapshot rows), `size` (row count), and `is_empty` (boolean).

trigger_keywords: write batch create operation wire format
how is a create encoded as a mutation
what precondition is added for create vs set
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A `create` operation encodes as an `update` mutation with a `currentDocument: {"exists": false}` precondition, plus the encoded fields. A `set` with `merge: true` encodes as an `update` mutation with an `updateMask` listing all top-level field paths, and no precondition. A plain `set` (no merge) encodes as an `update` mutation with no mask and no precondition. A `delete` encodes as a `delete` mutation with just the document name. The commit output contains `commit_result` (a list with one entry per write — an empty list `[]` per write in test mode) and `calls` listing the commit request.

trigger_keywords: JSON output formatting
how should the output be pretty printed
what indent level is used for output JSON
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All JSON output must be pretty-printed with 4-space indentation and a trailing newline character. Keys must preserve insertion order (no alphabetical resorting beyond what the domain model dictates). The top-level output for each command is a single JSON object printed to stdout.

trigger_keywords: how to encode client values to wire format
what wire key does a Python string map to
how are integers and floats encoded
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Client-to-wire encoding rules: Python `str` → `{"stringValue": ...}`, Python `int` → `{"integerValue": ...}`, Python `float` → `{"doubleValue": ...}`, Python `bool` → `{"booleanValue": ...}`, Python `None` → `{"nullValue": null}`, Python `list` → `{"arrayValue": {"values": [...]}}`, Python `dict` (plain) → `{"mapValue": {"fields": {...}}}`. Sentinel markers (SERVER_TIMESTAMP, DELETE) are extracted from the field map before encoding: server-timestamp fields are returned in a separate list and delete fields in another, neither appears in the encoded `fields` payload.

trigger_keywords: project structure requirements
should this be a single file or multiple files
how should the code be organized into modules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The implementation must be multi-file. Suggested layout: `src/` containing domain modules (`resource_paths.py`, `field_paths.py`, `value_mapping.py`, `snapshots.py`, `references.py`, `queries.py`, `writes.py`), an adapter layer (`adapter.py` or `handlers/`) responsible for JSON command routing, and a `main.py` entry point reading from stdin. Tests live under `tests/` or `rcb_tests/`. The core domain logic must have no dependency on stdin/stdout or JSON parsing — that belongs exclusively in the adapter layer.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: refer to the escaping logic from the query builder from the last sprint
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The field path segment escaping rules defined in Feature 2 (field_path_encoding): segments containing non-alphanumeric/underscore characters or starting with a digit must be backtick-quoted when rendered as wire mask strings; this same escaping is reused in query `select.fields[].fieldPath` and write `updateMask.fieldPaths[]` rendering.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The value encoding/decoding bidirectional contract from Feature 3 (value_mapping): specifically the typed descriptor objects for `referenceValue`, `timestampValue`, `geoPointValue`, and `bytesValue` which must be decoded into structured dicts with `type`/`kind` discriminator keys rather than raw primitives, and the sentinel extraction logic for SERVER_TIMESTAMP and DELETE markers.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have enough detail on that specific point in the spec. Could you check the test case JSON files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the relevant feature? The expected_output fields in those test cases are the authoritative contract for exact output shape. If it's an edge case not covered by the public tests, implement the most conservative/strict interpretation and flag it for review.

feature1_resource_names.json.description: Resource names can be composed, decomposed, classified, and compared using database-relative and absolute document paths.

feature2_field_path_encoding.json.description: Field paths can be constructed from path segments, parsed from dotted text, escaped for wire masks, and rejected when invalid.

feature3_value_mapping.json.description: Client values and wire values are converted bidirectionally, including nested containers, binary data, timestamps, geographic points, references, and sentinel values.

feature4_document_snapshots.json.description: Document snapshots expose immutable identity, existence, timestamps, full field data, and nested field lookup by textual or segmented field paths.

feature5_references_and_collections.json.description: Collection and document references expose stable identities, create child references, and list child collections through paged connection calls.

feature6_query_planning_and_results.json.description: Queries build structured requests for selection, filtering, ordering, limits, offsets, cursors, and decode returned document rows into snapshots.

feature7_write_batches.json.description: Write batches enqueue creates, sets, updates, deletes, transforms, preconditions, commits, and rollback requests as structured database mutations.
